items.q: What is hydraulic action in a river?
items.correct: The force of moving water itself compressing air into cracks in the river bank or bed, weakening rock until pieces break off — no sediment needed.
items.misconception: Hydraulic action is when rocks in the river smash into each other.
items.random_wrong: Hydraulic action is when the river dissolves rocks chemically.
items.why: Students mix up the four river erosion processes. Hydraulic action = water force; abrasion = water using rocks to scrape; attrition = rocks hitting rocks; solution (corrosion) = chemical dissolving.

items.correct: Longshore drift carries sediment past a change in coastline direction (e.g. river mouth); deposition continues into deeper water, building a curved depositional landform with a hook.
items.misconception: A spit forms where the river deposits its sediment at the coast.
items.random_wrong: A spit is a small island just off the coast.
items.why: Spits are coastal (longshore drift) deposits, not river deposits. The hook forms where wind shifts the current. Students confuse spits with deltas — different processes, different settings.

items.q: Why do glaciated valleys have a U-shape rather than a V-shape?
items.correct: Glaciers occupy the whole valley width, eroding the sides as well as the floor, producing steep sides and a flat floor (U-shape); rivers erode mainly downwards, leaving V-shape.
items.misconception: Glaciers cut U-shapes because ice is shaped like a U.
items.random_wrong: Glaciers carve V-shaped valleys just like rivers do.
items.why: The U-shape reflects the geometry of erosion (lateral plus vertical), not the shape of the ice itself. A river is confined to a thin channel and erodes vertically — a glacier fills the valley.

items.q: What does the natural warning of 'drawback' look like before a tsunami?
items.correct: The sea recedes far from shore
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The sea recedes far from shore. “Strong onshore winds” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many people died in the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami?
items.why: Correct answer: 230,000. “18,500” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate moment magnitude of the 2004 Sumatra-Andaman earthquake?
items.why: The real figure is 9.1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
